brief_document.title: Branch handle type and fork operation
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the BranchHandle type and the fork operation that creates it. A fork records the current root hash as the fork-point and allocates empty per-shard WAL buffers, sharing the parent's content-addressed tree nodes. This is the foundation for all branching operations — merge, time-travel, and pruning all build on the handle this brief creates.
brief_document.task: Define the BranchHandle type holding fork-point root hash, current root hash, and per-shard WAL buffer references. Implement fork() that records the current root hash and allocates empty WAL buffers without copying any tree nodes. Fork operates per-shard: each shard gets its own fork-point and branch buffer. Branch reads check the branch WAL buffer first, then the shared tree. This brief builds the fork primitive only — merge is BRANCH-002, snapshots are BRANCH-003.

requirements.title: Branch module structure
requirements.spec: Create the branch module with mod.rs containing only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. Create handle.rs and fork.rs as sub-modules.
acceptance: crates/haematite/src/branch/mod.rs exists with only pub mod and pub use statements
crates/haematite/src/branch/handle.rs exists
crates/haematite/src/branch/fork.rs exists
cargo check -p haematite succeeds

requirements.title: BranchHandle type
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a BranchHandle type that holds: the fork-point root hash (the common ancestor), the current root hash (initially equal to fork-point), and per-shard WAL buffer references for the branch's own writes. THE SYSTEM SHALL implement Debug and Clone for BranchHandle. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT store copies of tree nodes — the handle references the shared content-addressed store.
acceptance: BranchHandle has a fork_point() method returning the fork-point root hash
BranchHandle has a current_root() method returning the current root hash
BranchHandle has a shard_buffer(shard_id) method returning a reference to the branch's WAL buffer for that shard
On creation, current_root() equals fork_point()
BranchHandle implements Debug and Clone

requirements.title: Fork operation
requirements.spec: WHEN fork() is called on a database or shard, THE SYSTEM SHALL record the current committed root hash as the fork-point and allocate empty per-shard WAL buffers for the branch. THE SYSTEM SHALL complete in O(1) time regardless of database size — no tree node copying. THE SYSTEM SHALL return a BranchHandle.
acceptance: fork() returns a BranchHandle with fork_point equal to the current committed root hash
fork() allocates one empty WAL buffer per shard
fork() does not read or copy any tree nodes
fork() on a database with 1 million entries completes in constant time (no O(n) work)

requirements.title: Independent branch read-write
requirements.spec: WHILE a branch exists, THE SYSTEM SHALL allow reads and writes to the branch without affecting the parent. Writes append to the branch's per-shard WAL buffers. Reads check the branch WAL buffer first and fall through to the shared tree if the key is not in the buffer. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT propagate branch mutations to the parent's WAL buffer or tree.
acceptance: A put on the branch does not appear in a get on the parent
A put on the parent after fork does not appear in a get on the branch
A get on the branch returns the branch buffer value for keys written after fork
A get on the branch returns the parent tree value for keys not written after fork

requirements.title: Per-shard fork independence
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL fork each shard independently: each shard gets its own fork-point root hash and its own branch WAL buffer. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT create cross-shard coordination during fork. THE SYSTEM SHALL coordinate per-shard forks through the BranchHandle so callers interact with the branch as a single logical database.
acceptance: Each shard in the branch has an independent fork-point root hash
Each shard in the branch has an independent WAL buffer
A write to shard 3 on the branch does not affect shard 5 on the branch
BranchHandle routes gets/puts to the correct shard's branch buffer

requirements.title: BranchRegistry for tracking active branch roots
requirements.spec: Define a BranchRegistry type that maintains a HashSet of active branch root hashes. WHEN a BranchHandle is created via fork(), THE SYSTEM SHALL register its root hash in the registry. WHEN a BranchHandle is dropped, THE SYSTEM SHALL deregister its root hash. THE SYSTEM SHALL expose a live_roots() method returning the set of all active branch root hashes for use by the pruner (BRANCH-004).
acceptance: BranchRegistry::new() creates an empty registry
Forking a database registers the branch root in the registry
Dropping a BranchHandle deregisters its root
live_roots() returns all currently active branch root hashes
BranchRegistry is thread-safe (Arc<Mutex<HashSet<Hash>>> or equivalent)

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement merge or conflict resolution — those are BRANCH-002
SHALL NOT implement snapshot registry or time-travel — those are BRANCH-003
SHALL NOT implement snapshot pruning — that is BRANCH-004
SHALL NOT copy tree nodes during fork — fork is O(1) via shared content-addressed store
SHALL NOT implement branch commit (flushing branch WAL to tree) — that is part of merge in BRANCH-002
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests
cargo clippy -p haematite produces no errors
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/branch/ --include='*.rs' -l finds no non-test files
No file in crates/haematite/src/branch/ exceeds 500 lines

adrs.title: Content-addressed prolly tree over B+ tree with WAL overlay
adrs.decision: Content-addressed prolly tree over B+ tree with WAL overlay, because branching/merging/sync is the headline feature and must be structural (O(differences), history-independent) rather than overlaid (O(branch-writes), order-dependent). The rejected alternative (B+ tree + WAL overlay) makes branching an afterthought — functional but O(branch-writes) for merge and requiring WAL coordination for replication.
adrs.decided_by: Bono, Frodo Baggins, Sir Patick Stewart

checklist.text: BranchHandle type holds the fork-point root hash, current root hash, and per-shard WAL buffer references.
fork() records the current root hash as the fork-point and allocates empty per-shard WAL buffers without copying any tree nodes.
Fork completes in O(1) time regardless of database size (CN1).
A forked branch can be read and written independently of the parent — mutations on one do not affect the other.
Branch reads check the branch's WAL buffer first, then fall through to the shared content-addressed tree.
Fork operates per-shard: each shard's tree gets an independent fork-point and branch WAL buffer (CN5).

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to fork a database in constant time so that I can create experimental branches without waiting for data to copy.
As the Aion engine, I want to fork a workflow's event history so that I can replay against modified history for what-if debugging without affecting the live workflow.
As the Aion engine, I want to discard a branch without affecting the parent so that failed experiments leave no trace in the live database.
As a test environment, I want to fork production state into an isolated branch so that tests run against real data without risking production.
As a test environment, I want the fork to share tree nodes with production so that cloning a large database does not double the storage cost.

constraints.text: Fork must complete in O(1) time and O(1) space — no tree node copying, only root hash recording and empty buffer allocation.
Three-way merge must visit only subtrees where at least one hash differs across the three trees. Full-tree scan on merge is a correctness bug, not a performance issue.
Conflict resolution must never silently discard data. LWW is explicit lossy; vector-clock surfaces true conflicts; custom functions receive all three values.
Snapshot pruning must not delete tree nodes referenced by any active branch or named snapshot. Pruning a live reference is data loss.
Branch operations must work per-shard: fork = fork each shard's tree, merge = merge each shard's tree. No cross-shard locking or coordination.
Read-only checkout must not require WAL replay — it reads directly from the content-addressed node store at the specified root hash.

resolved_context.intention: Branching is the headline feature — the reason haematite exists instead of wrapping SQLite. When this cluster is complete, a developer can fork a database in constant time by sharing a root hash, diverge both sides with independent writes, and merge them back by walking only the subtrees that differ. Every committed root hash is a permanent, verifiable snapshot that can be checked out for read-only time-travel at any point in the future. The experience should feel like working with git branches — cheap to create, cheap to compare, cheap to merge — but for a live key-value database with concurrent writers. The content-addressed prolly tree from the core cluster is what makes this structural rather than bolted-on: identical subtrees share nodes, fork costs nothing, and merge cost is proportional to differences, not total data.
